items.q: What type of joint is the knee?
items.why: Students confuse knee with hip. Both are in the leg, but the hip is ball-and-socket (multi-directional); the knee is a hinge (mostly flexion-extension). Ball-and-socket joints have more range.

items.q: According to the inverted-U hypothesis, when is performance best?
items.correct: At an optimal moderate level of arousal.
items.why: Students assume 'more psyched up = better'. The inverted-U shows performance rises with arousal up to an optimum, then DECLINES with excess arousal (the athlete chokes). The optimum varies by skill.

items.q: Most movements in the human body use which class of lever?
items.why: Students assume the most familiar (first class - seesaw) is most common. Actually most body levers are third class (effort between fulcrum and load) - like the biceps curl. They sacrifice mechanical advantage for speed/range.

items.q: How many bones are in the adult human skeleton?
items.why: It's 206. “106” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Hip. “Elbow” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Number of bones in the adult human body?
items.why: The real figure is 206 bones. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
